findings.title: JSONDecodeError does not inherit from RequestException
findings.detail: The new `requests.JSONDecodeError` class inherits from `StandardJSONDecodeError` (json.JSONDecodeError or ValueError) and `SimpleJSONDecodeError` (simplejson.JSONDecodeError or Exception), but NOT from `RequestException`. The PR description explicitly states that `requests.RequestException` should catch this error, but that is impossible with the current inheritance chain. Users who write `except requests.RequestException` will NOT catch a `requests.JSONDecodeError`. This breaks the documented behaviour and is a backwards-incompatibility surprise since all other `requests` exceptions are `RequestException` subtypes. It should inherit from `RequestException` in addition to (or instead of being placed before) the JSON error base classes.

findings.title: JSON decode errors inside the UTF-detection branch are silently swallowed
findings.detail: In the `json()` method, when a UTF encoding is successfully detected and `json.loads()` is called on `self.content.decode(encoding)`, only `UnicodeDecodeError` is caught. If `json.loads` raises `json.JSONDecodeError` in this branch (e.g., the content is valid UTF-8 but not valid JSON), the exception propagates as a raw `json.JSONDecodeError` rather than being wrapped in `requests.JSONDecodeError`. This is inconsistent with the behaviour in the fallback branch (lines 915-920) and defeats the purpose of this PR. A `json.JSONDecodeError` handler should also be added in the inner try/except at line 904-913.

findings.title: Name clash: local parameter `json` shadows the imported `json` module
findings.detail: In `prepare_body(self, data, files, json=None)`, the parameter named `json` shadows the module-level `from .compat import json` import (line 43). Line 476 calls `json.dumps(json, allow_nan=False)` — here the first `json` refers to the parameter (the data to serialise), not the module, so `json.dumps` fails with `AttributeError` at runtime when the `json` parameter is not `None`. This bug existed before this PR (`complexjson` was used previously), and the PR preserves and makes it worse by renaming the import from `complexjson` to `json`, creating a direct clash with the function parameter name. The import alias `complexjson` was specifically chosen to avoid this clash. Reverting the rename back to `complexjson` (or choosing another alias) is necessary.

findings.title: Integration tests make live HTTP network calls and require simplejson
findings.detail: Both tests hit external URLs (`https://httpbin.org/get` and `https://google.com`) via real HTTP requests. This makes the tests flaky (network-dependent), slow, and unsuitable for a standard CI run. The existing test suite uses mocking or a local test server (see `tests/testserver/`). Additionally, `import simplejson` at line 2 will cause an `ImportError` and the entire test module to fail when `simplejson` is not installed, yet the PR claims to work without it. Unit tests using `unittest.mock` or `responses` library with a pre-baked invalid-JSON response body would be correct here.

findings.title: test_json_decode_failure_catch makes four separate network requests
findings.detail: Each `with pytest.raises(...)` block independently calls `get(failure_url).json()`, resulting in four live network requests to `https://google.com` in a single test function. Even setting aside the network dependency issue, this pattern is fragile (Google may return JSON in future, or rate-limit the test runner). The four `pytest.raises` blocks should be combined into one request whose result is checked for all exception types using `isinstance`.

findings.title: simplejson.JSONDecodeError fallback to bare Exception is too broad
findings.detail: When `simplejson` is not installed, `SimpleJSONDecodeError` is set to `Exception`. Because `JSONDecodeError` inherits from `Exception` (via `SimpleJSONDecodeError`) it means `except Exception` now has to be placed in the MRO, which can obscure the class hierarchy and makes `isinstance(e, Exception)` trivially true for everything. A sentinel private exception class (e.g. `class _NoSimplejsonJSONDecodeError(Exception): pass`) would be far safer, or simply omit the simplejson base when it isn't installed. Using the raw `Exception` base makes catch-all error handling ambiguous and may cause unexpected MRO issues.

findings.title: Python 2 compatibility comment is misleading
findings.detail: The comment `# Python 2, ValueError is raised` on line 13 implies Python 2's `json` module raises `ValueError`, but the actual reason the `ImportError` occurs is that `json.JSONDecodeError` did not exist as a named class in Python 2 (it was added in Python 3.5). The comment should say something like `# Python < 3.5 does not have json.JSONDecodeError` to be technically accurate.

findings.title: simplejson no longer imported in compat.py but json is still exported from there
findings.detail: The PR removes the `try: import simplejson as json` block but keeps `import json` at the top of `compat.py` and `models.py` imports it via `from .compat import json`. This works correctly, but it is inconsistent style: `json` is not listed alongside the other compat imports in the `from .compat import (...)` block in `models.py` — it gets a dedicated `from .compat import json` line (line 43). Since `json` is now just the stdlib module and no longer a compatibility shim, it should be imported directly in `models.py` (`import json`) rather than routed through `compat`.
